title: Qwen on local mini PC (Beelink) #ai #localai #coding #hermesagent #openclaw
summary: Keith AI demonstrates running Qwen language model locally on a Beelink mini PC. The video covers setup and execution of local LLM inference on edge hardware with reference to related frameworks and agents.
writeup: A hands-on test of the Beelink SER10 Max OpenClaw Edition mini PC as a local inference platform for Hermes and OpenClaw, motivated by cloud credit costs running $10 to $20 per day. The device ships pre-configured with llama.cpp and a quantized 3.6B model, promising immediate setup, but real-world multi-agent and tool-call workloads expose a hard ceiling: 12 tokens per second. The through-line is honest cost accounting: local hardware is not free, and the practical conclusion is a hybrid strategy, local inference as the default, cloud fallback when speed or complexity demands it.
key_points: Running Hermes and OpenClaw in the cloud was costing roughly $10 to $20 USD per day in credits, making local inference economically attractive for privacy-sensitive agent workflows.
The Beelink SER10 Max OpenClaw Edition ships with llama.cpp and a quantized 3.6B model pre-installed, allowing the system to be operational within minutes of unboxing.

Under real agentic workloads, complex tool calls and multi-agent chains, the local quantized setup achieved only 12 tokens per second, which becomes a significant bottleneck when sub-agents chain sequentially.
The author previously tested local inference on a Jetson Nano, and the SER10 represents a second data point in that personal comparison, not a first experiment with local LLMs.
The recommended architecture is local-first with cloud fallback: local inference handles routine and privacy-sensitive tasks while cloud APIs absorb the latency-sensitive or compute-heavy work.
